posts.name: Y@0 C <aitch> 00 ng
posts.cooked:
My best friend flew into town, his first flight since the start of the pandemic, so we could revisit a restaurant that we haven’t been to in many years: the superlative, at times quirky, but always lovable Lazy Bear in SF. This was one of those nights where everything fell into place, the food and the wines and the companionship taking us out of ourselves and showing us a new world of flavors and sensations that we were happy to explore like giddy children. The kind of evening that reminds you why we even do this in the first place, and renews your enthusiasm to do more

1979 Jacques Tremblay Chablis 'Fourchaume’
Never heard of Jacques Tremblay in Chablis but we decided to take a complete flyer on it off the Lazy Bear list; poured a somewhat brownish gold but the color cleared up beautifully over a couple of hours; starts with a nutty, faintly spicy, almost aged Champagne aroma, before giving way to a big, stunning hit of sweet exotic fruit - even pineapple - on the palate, that made me exclaim ‘Wow, it’s good!’; on the finish it seems to summon a big edifice of chalky, seashell minerality out of thin air, as if to remind you of its roots in Chalis; the flavors gain scale and persistence with air, bringing out a rich lactic, even buttery note after several hours; a complete and satisfying wine, one that the sommelier mentioned that he had been walking past and painstakingly inventorying for the past three years, wondering the whole time what it would taste like; we were very glad to have the chance to find out with him; very good

(pic below)

2004 Chateau Rayas
Gently double decanted via syphon 1h before dinner; tastiest sediment I’ve ever had from a red wine; beautiful crystalline strawberry and raspberry flavors in the foreground, with a touch of something foresty and herbal at the end - the ‘white pepper’ often said to be the Rayas signature - and then once it’s in your mouth you get delicate florals wafting up the back of your nose; ebullient and ethereal on the palate, a gateway to another world; we tried a lot of different foods with it: spicy, sweet, bitter, earthy, briny, and there was nothing that could disturb its seemingly perfect poise as it sailed on into the infinite; yet there is absolutely nothing shouty or attention grabbing about this, and if one weren’t primed to demand greatness by the reputation or price, one might just chug the lot without remarking on any of the above qualities; it got us talking about the massive guts it would take for a lesser-known producer to make wine in this style, because you really have to concentrate and focus to appreciate the greatness on display here; did get a bit darker and blockier with several hours of air, losing some of the elegance in the process, so I wouldn’t be too aggressive on decanting; very good plus, wine of the year so far

1950 Chateau Gilette Sauternes
Dark brown, well preserved; apricot, spice, and a touch of white mushroom; has ‘swallowed its sugar’ and is light and graceful on the palate, if not a patch on the other two wines; decent plus

posts.name: Dennis Atick
posts.cooked:
Well done. That’s a gem of a Chablis to pull of a wine list and to have a nice showing is a plus!
I don’t know the restaurant at all, but that’s a cool find.
Can’t go wrong with Rayas.

posts.display_username: M a r k K a p l a n

posts.name: Y@0 C <aitch> 00 ng
posts.cooked:
Nice Yao! I was at Lazy Bear 3 weeks ago but didn’t drink nearly as exotically as yourself. I’m curious what your grading scale is. You drink a lot of nice wines, and if your woty merits a very good+, what comes above it?

You flatter me

My scale tops out at Excellent, for wines that trigger strong emotions, memories, images. One of those things where you know it when you experience it. Only six wines to date but hopefully more in the future. Below Excellent I have Very Good (made to a high, even superlative, standard but doesn’t move me per the above), Good (plenty of pleasure but I find myself nitpicking here and there), Decent (quaffable but no more), and Yuck (do not put in mouth)

You flatter me

My scale tops out at Excellent, for wines that trigger strong emotions, memories, images. One of those things where you know it when you experience it. Only six wines to date but hopefully more in the future. Below Excellent I have Very Good (made to a high, even superlative, standard but doesn’t move me per the above), Good (plenty of pleasure but I find myself nitpicking here and there), Decent (quaffable but no more), and Yuck (do not put in mouth)

Now to me that’s a manageable rating system. I love the concept.
For myself I have Outstanding above Excellent. And modifiers for value, and above (or below!) expectations.

posts.name: Kris_P@tten
posts.cooked:
2005 is a tannic monster. I drank one young after release and it was delicious, then it shut down hard. I’d only open one if you drink it in an hour or open it and leave it on counter to slowly breathe for 12 hours, 24 is too much IMO.

Rayas is just Rayas…nothing in the world like it…a snowflake in the wine world.

Also just wants to mention that if you have a 6-pack, then go for it! Try one out. Even though it might not be perfect, it is probably still an absolute treat! The one i had a year ago was really good and was only opened a few hours before we served it and then we just followed it in the bottle. The nose alone on these wines can take you to heaven

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Recommendation of the winemaker: open 24 h in advance …
my rec: + then decant 1-2 h in advance …
this way it should be very enjoyable

I have heard of this quite a bit with Rayas (and most Reynaud wines), but it scares me for some reason. I’m scared the wine will get a little too much air…

Don’t be scared of opening 24 hours in advance! It invariably works wonders for these wines. Just don’t let the wine get too warm while it’s open: I open them in the cellar and leave them there.

posts.name: Jonathan Loesberg
posts.cooked:
Don’t be scared of opening 24 hours in advance! It invariably works wonders for these wines. Just don’t let the wine get too warm while it’s open: I open them in the cellar and leave them there.

So dont recork it during that 24 hrs?

Pour out a taste, recork and leave in the cellar.

Reynaud’s advice with his wines is to open, pour out a small amount and let breathe, opened, for twenty-four hours. He gives this advice as well for his Chateau des Tours Vacqueyras and Côte du Rhone. I don’t have the means to drink Rayas frequently enough to have an opinion on this method. With both the Vacqueyras and the CDR, I find that 24 hours mellows it out too much for my taste. I prefer to decant for 3-4 hours, which gets the effect he is looking for, but to a slightly lesser degree. The advice applies as well to his white wines. And he says to do it with his older wines as well (it’s sort of hard not too, these days, since he releases many of them only after 10 years).

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:

Reynaud’s advice with his wines is to open, pour out a small amount and let breathe, opened, for twenty-four hours. He gives this advice as well for his Chateau des Tours Vacqueyras and Côte du Rhone. I don’t have the means to drink Rayas frequently enough to have an opinion on this method. With both the Vacqueyras and the CDR, I find that 24 hours mellows it out too much for my taste. I prefer to decant for 3-4 hours, which gets the effect he is looking for, but to a slightly lesser degree. The advice applies as well to his white wines. And he says to do it with his older wines as well (it’s sort of hard not too, these days, since he releases many of them only after 10 years).

Well, he advised me to recork… unless I misunderstood.

posts.name: Jonathan Loesberg
posts.cooked:

Reynaud’s advice with his wines is to open, pour out a small amount and let breathe, opened, for twenty-four hours. He gives this advice as well for his Chateau des Tours Vacqueyras and Côte du Rhone. I don’t have the means to drink Rayas frequently enough to have an opinion on this method. With both the Vacqueyras and the CDR, I find that 24 hours mellows it out too much for my taste. I prefer to decant for 3-4 hours, which gets the effect he is looking for, but to a slightly lesser degree. The advice applies as well to his white wines. And he says to do it with his older wines as well (it’s sort of hard not too, these days, since he releases many of them only after 10 years).

Well, he advised me to recork… unless I misunderstood.

I’m sure you meet him more than I do. I only met him once when he happened to be at Chateau des Tours when I was there. But he did say that, as do the people who run des Tours to anyone who asks. It does seem to me that opening and recorking after only a taste is extracted would have a very limited effect. But, again, I have very little experience with Rayas and couldn’t say.

posts.name: larry schaffer
posts.cooked:
Thanks for all of the notes. Slow oxing or decanting is always a bit of a crap shoot. It’s tough to compare/contrast methodologies on bottles like these - they’re too precious and you don’t want to be wrong

I prefer to not decant nearly any wine and just enjoy watching its slow evolution - it takes a great deal if patience but making wine and having 3 kids has made me much more so . . .

Cheers.

posts.name: Marcus Goodfellow
posts.cooked:
Thanks for all of the notes. Slow oxing or decanting is always a bit of a crap shoot. It’s tough to compare/contrast methodologies on bottles like these - they’re too precious and you don’t want to be wrong

I prefer to not decant nearly any wine and just enjoy watching its slow evolution - it takes a great deal if patience but making wine and having 3 kids has made me much more so . . .

Cheers.

I have come to this as well(although it has taken only two children). We routinely drink wines over 2-4 days, and if a wine is showing well it can easily be drunk sooner. If it’s tight we’ll just recork it and leave it be.

posts.name: Robert Sand
posts.cooked:
Thanks for all of the notes. Slow oxing or decanting is always a bit of a crap shoot. It’s tough to compare/contrast methodologies on bottles like these - they’re too precious and you don’t want to be wrong

I know of no wine of good quality that cannot stand a few hours of slow oxing - and for many 1, 2, 3 days are no problem at all.
Just the opposite: a few hours are usually for the benefit.

(it takes quite a lot of patience to follow Rayas over 24+ hours …)
